:::{#lem-ph-event-chain-rule}
#### Chain-rule split of an event factor

Under the no-ties assumption, the probability that subject $(i)$ fails at $t_i$,
given the risk set $R(t_i)$, factors into a
*which-subject-fails* factor and a *whether-a-failure-occurs* factor:

$$
\Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}
= \underbrace{\Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails} \mid 1 \text{ failure at } t_i,\ R(t_i)}}_{\text{which subject fails}}
\;\times\;
\underbrace{\Pr\sb{1 \text{ failure at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}}_{\text{whether a failure occurs}} .
$$ {#eq-ph-chain-rule}
:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

::: {.proof}
Let

- $A =$ "subject $(i)$ fails at $t_i$",
- $B =$ "exactly one failure occurs at $t_i$",
- $C =$ "the risk set at $t_i$ is $R(t_i)$".

By the chain rule of probability, $\Pr(A \cap B \mid C) = \Pr(A \mid B \cap C)\,\Pr(B \mid C)$.
Under the no-ties assumption $A \subset B$
(subject $(i)$ failing at $t_i$ is automatically a one-failure event),
so $A \cap B = A$ and therefore
$\Pr(A \mid C) = \Pr(A \mid B \cap C)\,\Pr(B \mid C)$,
which is @eq-ph-chain-rule.
:::

:::{#exm-ph-event-chain-rule}
#### Splitting the event probability numerically

Continue the running scenario of @exm-ph-single-subj-fail,
in which subject $2$ is the one who fails at $t_i = 4$.
The left side of @eq-ph-chain-rule is subject $2$'s single-subject failure probability,
$q^*_2(4) = 0.10$ (@exm-ph-single-subj-fail),
and the whether-a-failure factor is $0.30$ (@exm-ph-whether-failure).
The chain rule therefore pins the which-subject factor at

$$
\Pr\sb{2 \text{ fails} \mid 1 \text{ failure at } 4,\ R(4)}
= \frac{\Pr\sb{2 \text{ fails at } 4 \mid R(4)}}{\Pr\sb{1 \text{ failure at } 4 \mid R(4)}}
\approx \frac{0.10}{0.30} = \frac{1}{3} ,
$$

and indeed $0.10 \approx \tfrac{1}{3} \times 0.30$ reproduces the left side.
:::
